abstract.t: This document defines an information modeling language designed to improve the rigor of Internet technical specifications. Existing standards like ABNF, XSD, and JSON Schema define formal syntax for text and structured data values. But while RFC 3444 and RFC 8477 establish goals for information modeling, adoption remains limited. JSON Abstract Data Notation (JADN) addresses this by defining a formal syntax for information values using a uniform type structure that balances simplicity with extensibility and expressive power. The key feature of any IM is equivalence across data formats, and unlike traditional schema languages, a JADN schema is itself an information value. This ensures equivalence across schema representations, allowing authors to select or design a representation suitable for the application and target audience.

section.name: Introduction
section.t: begins: "There has been ongoing confusion about the differences between Information Models and Data Models for defining managed objects in network management." The confusion can be partially attributed to the absence of a definition of information itself, so this specification begins with two:

dl.dd: structured, processed data that has been given context, meaning, and semantics within a specific domain. It turns raw facts into usable knowledge by organizing data objects, their attributes, and their relationships, typically at a high level of abstraction, independent of physical storage protocols.
the reduction of uncertainty, measured as the statistical rarity of a message rather than its semantic meaning. It quantifies how much a data point reduces doubt, measured in bits.

section.t: Given a data value, the information it conveys can be viewed as the questions it answers. An information model (IM) defines equivalence between messages, and a concise message such as an RFC 791 IP packet contains the identical information (both semantics: "What is the source address?" and its entropy: "32 bits") as an equivalent verbose message in JSON or XML format.
An implementable IM specification must also define Information Value, Data Value, and the relationships between them:

dl.dd: the value of a variable (a typed unit of state) in a processing environment. In this specification information values are displayed with strings in single quotes to avoid confusion with similar-looking JSON data values.

a fixed sequence of bytes usable outside a processing environment. Also known as a Document, Message, File, Stream, Literal, or Lexical Value. Because messages are immutable they can be compared for equality and hashed or signed for integrity.

definition of the content of an information or data value of that type. An IM type has three properties: an information value space, a literal space for each data format, and a small set of functions and relations including ordering of the value space and mappings from each literal space to the value space. This definition extends the XML Schema Datatype to information modeling by including the literal spaces and mappings for multiple data formats. Both information and data values are instances of Type.

the implementation of a variable in a processing environment. An object has a type, a current value, and the behavior defined for variables of that type. Objects are instances of Class. Note that this is not specific to object-oriented programming languages: K&R C has an "int" type and its implementation of int variables (the int class) has behavior such as addition and comparison in addition to the value space defined by the int type. Also note that JavaScript defines Boolean, String, Number, Array, Map and Set objects in chapters 20-24. JSON confusingly uses "object" to refer only to the values of Map objects, not any others. Objects in this specification have values of all types as in JavaScript, not just Maps as in JSON.

a named equivalence mapping between information values and literals. More than one data format can be defined for a single media type, for example Record information values can be represented as either Map or Array literals in the same media type depending on data format.
Two data values are equivalent if and only if they are the same type and are mapped to the same information value.

section.t: For an IM to be useful it must be abstract and language-independent. An abstract schema is a high level specification that separates information sharing requirements from implementation details. This is desirable for initial conceptual design where details are unknown or undecided, and for exposition and publication where they are distracting. But robust implementation requires unambiguous specification of schema-independent encoding rules to translate between information values and messages. An IM defines both information values and message representations in a way that does not depend on programming language or data format.
Using an IM can support system development in three ways:

ol.li: Pseudocode: describes message content at a high level without mechanically generating or processing messages
Template: generates concrete schemas (data models) more consistently than writing them directly, while reusing existing message processing software

IM: validates and processes message content directly, enabling lossless translation among data formats. This specification defines the required observable result of lossless round-trip translation, but not a required algorithm for achieving that result.

section.name: Examples
section.t: The following example representations of a simple GPS Coordinate type definition illustrate that a JADN schema is an information value, that the information value is identical regardless of data format, and what that value looks like when represented as different data values. The formats shown are:

ul.li: JSON data: the normative storage format for JADN schemas
XML data: an XML format composed of JADN elements, called XML Abstract Schema Definition (XASD) language.
Text: JADN Information Definition Language (IDL), an example non-normative domain-specific language (DSL).
Property Tables: a popular data standards publication format, shown as Markdown source. Tables in HTML or XML markup formats are also possible.

figure.name: JADN XASD Format
figure.artwork: <?xml version="1.0" ?> <Schema version="2.0"> <Types> <Type name="Coordinate" type="Record">GPS Coordinate <Field fid="1" fname="latitude" type="Number" minInclusive="-90.0" maxInclusive="90.0">Latitude</Field> <Field fid="2" fname="longitude" type="Number" minExclusive="-180.0" maxInclusive="180.0">Longitude</Field> </Type> </Types> </Schema>

figure.name: JADN IDL Format
figure.artwork: Coordinate = Record // GPS Coordinate 1 latitude Number=[-90.0, 90.0] // Latitude 2 longitude Number=(-180.0, 180.0] // Longitude

figure.name: JADN Property Table, Markdown Format
figure.artwork:
**Type: Coordinate (Record):** GPS Coordinate
| ID | Name | Type | \# | Description | |----|---------------|------------------------|----|-------------| | 1 | **latitude** | Number=[-90.0, 90.0] | 1 | Latitude | | 2 | **longitude** | Number=(-180.0, 180.0] | 1 | Longitude |

section.name: Requirements Language
section.t: The key words "MUST", "MUST NOT", "REQUIRED", "SHALL", "SHALL NOT", "SHOULD", "SHOULD NOT", "RECOMMENDED", "NOT RECOMMENDED", "MAY", and "OPTIONAL" in this document are to be interpreted as described in BCP 14 when, and only when, they appear in all capitals, as shown here.

section.name: Types
section.t: A JADN schema is composed exclusively of type definitions, not a mixture of types and other mechanisms sometimes called classes in other data modeling approaches. Every type definition has the identical five-element structure:

ol.li: TypeName: the name of the type being defined.
CoreType: The JADN built-in type of the type being defined.
TypeOptions: a set of zero or more TypeOption values applicable to the CoreType (conditional).
Fields: an array of zero or more Item or Field definitions as applicable to the CoreType (conditional).

section.t: This uniform type structure along with a namespaced package header makes a JADN IM capable yet easy to process. Currently-defined TypeOptions and FieldOptions provide significant expressive power, and unlimited space for new options enables easy extensibility.

JADN IM types are structured as a directed acyclic graph (DAG). Definitions can be arranged semi-hierarchically for readability, and common types can be reused in multiple types. This differs from tree graphs where types must always be acyclic and denormalized, and from mesh graphs that make no distinction between contained and referenced content. Cross-references (links / foreign keys) in a DAG support cyclic relationships where needed, but are the exception rather than the rule. Data must live somewhere, and only an IM that distinguishes values from references specifies where in a document or message to find it.

The types in this specification are well-known abstract concepts such as "integer", "sequence" and "map". They are not exhaustively defined here because many other publications provide excellent definitions of these concepts. However, this specification will attempt to describe these types well enough that they can be readily recognized and distinguished from other abstractions with which they may be confused.
JADN defines information values and message syntax based on twelve Core types in three categories:

ul.li: Primitive: Five types where each instance is an atomic value. The Fields array is empty in primitive type definitions.

Compound: Five types where each instance is a collection of values. The Fields array is empty for unstructured compound types (ArrayOf, MapOf) and has the same five-element Field structure for all structured compound types (Array, Map, Record).

Union: Two types where each instance is selected from a set of possible values. The Fields array is a three-element Item structure for the Enumerated type, and the five-element Field structure for the Choice type.

section.t: The Item structure for Enumerated types is:
The Field structure for Compound and Choice types is:

section.name: Options
section.t: lists all TypeOptions and FieldOptions defined in this specification. Their meaning and usage is described under specific types in the following sections. Each option has a numeric ID, a name, and a value type. For brevity, options are serialized in JSON format as a list of strings using "TaggedString" encoding. In TaggedStrings, the Unicode codepoint of the first character is the option ID and the remaining characters are the string representation of its value.

In the GPS Coordinate example, latitude has options represented in JSON format as ["y-90.0", "z90.0"]. From the first character "y" (U+0079 = ID 121) has name "minInclusive", the remaining characters are the value string "-90.0", and the type "BType" indicates that the value type is the base type of the field (Number). The information value of the latitude options is {'minInclusive': -90.0, 'maxInclusive': 90.0}:
section.sourcecode: +----+-----------+ TaggedString TypeOption = "y-90.0" (String) | ID | Value | ID = 121 = U+0079 = lower case "y" +----+-----------+ Info: Name = "minInclusive", Value = -90.0 (Number)
section.t: The more verbose XASD representation of latitude options: <type="Number" minInclusive="-90.0" maxInclusive="90.0"> is equivalent to the to the JSON representation because both are mapped to the same information value.

figure.name: TypeOptions and FieldOptions
figure.artwork:
ID TypeOption Type ID Description ___ __________ _______ ___ __________________________________________ 61 id Boolean =: Fields indexed by FieldId, not FieldName 42 valueType String *: Value for ArrayOf and MapOf is referenced type 43 keyType String +: Key for MapOf is referenced type 35 enum String #: Enumeration derived from referenced type 62 pointer String >: Pointers derived from referenced type 37 pattern String %: String matches Regular expression 117 default BType u: Default value 118 const BType v: Constant value 119 minExclusive BType w: minimum value, excluding bound 120 maxExclusive BType x: maximum value, excluding bound 121 minInclusive BType y: minimum value 122 maxInclusive BType z: maximum value 123 minLength Integer {: minimum string length or collection count 125 maxLength Integer }: maximum string length or collection count 113 unique Boolean q: ArrayOf instance is an OrderedSet 115 set Boolean s: ArrayOf instance is a Set 98 unordered Boolean b: ArrayOf instance is a Bag 111 sequence Boolean o: Map, MapOf, Record instance is an OrderedSet 67 combine String C: Choice instance is an untagged union 47 format String /: Semantic validation keyword 69 scale Integer E: Fixed point scale factor, value * 10^n 116 tagString String t: Table for tag-string serialization 97 abstract Boolean a: Type is not instantiable as a value 102 final Boolean f: Type cannot have subtypes 101 extends String e: Type is a superset of referenced type 114 restricts String r: Type is a subset of referenced type 65 attr Boolean A: Data value may be an XML attribute
ID FieldOption Type ID Description ___ __________ _______ ___ __________________________________________ 91 minOccurs Integer [: min cardinality, default = 1 93 maxOccurs Integer ]: max cardinality, default = 1 48 nillable Boolean 0: Instance may have no value 38 tagId Integer &: field that specifies tagged union type 60 dir Boolean <: pointer enum treats field as collection 75 key Boolean K: field is the primary key for TypeName 76 link Boolean L: field links to an instance of FieldType

section.name: Primitive Types
section.t: A primitive type has no substructure, and specifies an unrestricted space of atomic values without regard to processing mechanisms or data format. Options applicable to each type limit the value space; if more than one option is present the value space is their intersection.
JADN defines the following primitive types:

ul.li: Boolean: a value from the set of two possible states.
Integer: a value from the discrete whole number line.
Number: a value from the continuous real number line.

section.name: Integer
section.t: An integer is often said to be a number with no fractional part. Integer and number are distinct types, both mathematically and computationally, because a countably infinite set of discrete values is different from a continuum. 1.01 has a fractional part, as does 1.00000000001, as does 1 + 10^-n for every non-infinite positive n. For this reason every decimal character string and every IEEE-754 floating point value that represents an integer, regardless of precision, also represents an infinite number of values with a fractional part. It is impossible for the number type to represent an integer without consuming infinite resources.

section.name: Packages
section.t: A JADN package is the mechanism used to organize IM types into groups. It serves as a namespace, meaning types within a package must have unique names, and supports modularization by breaking down large models into manageable subunits, including libraries of sharable types.
JADN packaging is based on XML namespaces:

ul.li: A namespace is identified by a Uniform Resource Identifier (URI), which is a unique name, not necessarily a link to a network resource.

section.name: Shortcuts
section.t: JADN defines a set of core definitions plus several shortcuts that make IM schemas more adaptable or less redundant. Shortcuts are syntactic sugar implemented using options that can be replaced with additional types without affecting functionality. Expanding shortcuts into core definitions simplifies serialization and validation code, and viewing the expanded schema may aid understanding of the IM.
The shortcuts and the options used to implement them are:

section.name: Anonymous Types
section.t: This shortcut allows fields within a structured type to be defined without a TypeName. Expanding the definition generates an explicitly named type for each anonymous field, moves all TypeOptions included in FieldOptions to the generated type, and replaces FieldType with the TypeName of the generated type. This requires FieldType to be a non-structured CoreType and any TypeOption values included in FieldOptions to apply to FieldType.
Example: a structured type with anonymous fields:
section.sourcecode: Coordinate = Record // A GPS coordinate 1 latitude Number=[-90.0, 90.0] // A Number between -90 and 90 degrees 2 longitude Number=(-180.0, 180.0] // A Number between -180 and 180 degrees
section.t: Expanding the type generates two named types and replaces FieldType with their names. This requires a naming convention for generating path names as discussed in . Writing an IM with explicitly named types (e.g., "Longitude") avoids the need for path names.
section.sourcecode:
Coordinate = Record // A GPS coordinate 1 latitude Coordinate.latitude // A Number between -90 and 90 degrees 2 longitude Coordinate.longitude // A Number between -180 and 180 degrees
Coordinate.latitude = Number=[-90.0, 90.0] Coordinate.longitude = Number=(-180.0, 180.0]

section.name: Schema Serialization
section.t: ...non-normative examples...

section.name: Simple Unordered (Bullet) List
section.t: Text before the list
Text after the list.

section.name: Ordered List With Lowercase Letters in Brackets Instead of Numbers
Continuous Numbering in a List That is Split by Text or Sections
section.t: Text to introduce the list
Some text in between

section.name: Section in-between
section.t: More text in-between

section.name: Lists With Hanging Labels
section.t: the list item is indented the value of indent

dl.dd: With a label shorter than the indent.
With a label longer than the indent.

section.name: Source Code Examples
section.t: This is an example C program

section.name: Using xref
section.t: A reference to
A reference to

section.name: IANA Considerations
section.t: This memo includes no request to IANA.

section.name: Security Considerations
section.t: This document should not affect the security of the Internet.

front.title: XML Schema Definition Language (XSD) 1.1 Part 2: Datatypes

section.t: This becomes an Appendix
